target.title: fix(diagnostics): carry session UUID on interactive dispatch events
target.body:
## Why

Plugins and exporters that subscribe to diagnostic events via `onDiagnosticEvent` need the canonical session UUID — the same id OpenClaw's JSONL session transcript writes as its session-header `id` field (`src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/bootstrap.ts`) — so downstream systems can join diagnostic/telemetry rows back to the transcript by id.

The **cron / isolated-agent** path already provides it: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` passes `sessionId: prepared.context.runSessionId` into `createDiagnosticMessageLifecycle`, so `message.queued` and `session.state` events carry the UUID.

The **interactive dispatch** path does not. `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` creates the diagnostic lifecycle with `sessionKey` only — even though the canonical UUID is available from the session store entry (`resolveSessionStoreLookup(ctx, cfg).entry?.sessionId`, the same source already used a few lines later to resolve `operationSessionId`). As a result every interactive `message.queued` / `session.state` event emits `sessionId: undefined`, and consumers cannot join those events to the transcript by UUID.

This is an asymmetry/oversight, not a design choice — there is no config that turns it on.

## Change

Pass `sessionId: initialSessionStoreEntry.entry?.sessionId` into the interactive `createDiagnosticMessageLifecycle({...})` call. The `resolveSessionStoreLookup(ctx, cfg)` lookup (read-only; needs only `ctx` + `cfg`, both already in scope) is hoisted a few lines up so its result is available at lifecycle-creation time. The lifecycle's existing `resolveRef` then defaults `sessionId` onto every emitted event (`message.queued`, `session.state`, `message.processed`), matching the cron path.

Safety: when the store entry exists, `entry?.sessionId` equals the UUID written to the JSONL session header. When it is absent (brand-new session not yet persisted), it is `undefined`, which preserves today's `sessionKey`-only behavior — the change never emits a *mismatched* UUID. This mirrors the existing canonical resolution (`operationSessionId = ... ?? initialSessionStoreEntry.entry?.sessionId ?? ...`).

## Files

- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` — hoist `initialSessionStoreEntry` lookup above the lifecycle creation; pass `sessionId` (+2/−1).
- `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` — new test asserting interactive diagnostic events carry the session-store UUID.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** On the interactive dispatch path, diagnostic events (`message.queued`, `session.state`, `message.processed`) were emitted with `sessionId: undefined`, so a plugin subscribing via `onDiagnosticEvent` could not join them to the JSONL session transcript by UUID. After this patch they carry the canonical session UUID, matching the cron path.

**Real environment tested:** A deployed OpenClaw gateway (v2026.4.29) built from this branch on a Linux host, running a live Telegram channel with an `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` agent. Diagnostic spans exported via OTLP through a running OpenTelemetry collector into Grafana Tempo.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the runtime from this branch into the gateway `dist/`, restarted the gateway service, sent a real message to the agent over Telegram (session `agent:main:main`), then queried the trace store with `curl`:
`curl -sG "$TEMPO/api/search" --data-urlencode 'q={ .prov.harness="openclaw" && .prov.session.uuid != "" }'`, then fetched the returned trace and read its span attributes. Gateway events were read live with `journalctl -u openclaw-gateway -f`.

**After-fix evidence:** Live runtime output (redacted). The interactive turn ran its full lifecycle in the gateway log:
```
[agentweave-bridge] event: message.queued      sessionKey: agent:main:main source: dispatch
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.call.started  sessionKey: agent:main:main
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.call.completed sessionKey: agent:main:main
[agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage         sessionKey: agent:main:main
[agentweave-bridge] event: message.processed    sessionKey: agent:main:main
```
The resulting `openclaw.turn` span, fetched from Tempo (`curl .../api/traces/<id>`), now carries the UUID — it carried none on this path before the patch:
```
span: openclaw.turn
  prov.harness      = openclaw
  prov.session.id   = agent:main:main                       (route key, unchanged)
  prov.session.key  = agent:main:main
  prov.session.uuid = 4767da88-20d1-459d-b1d6-60ecd493b0fa   (canonical session UUID, now present)
```
`4767da88-…` is the same UUID OpenClaw writes to the JSONL session-header `id`, so the diagnostic stream / span can now be joined to the transcript by id.

**Observed result after fix:** The interactive turn completed normally and its diagnostic events + telemetry span carry `sessionId` / `prov.session.uuid` equal to the canonical session UUID. Before the patch this field was absent on the interactive path; `prov.session.id` (the route key) is unchanged, so parent-linking is unaffected. No other behavior changed.

**What was not tested:** The brand-new-session case where no store entry exists yet — the lookup returns `undefined` and the event keeps today's `sessionKey`-only behavior. That path is covered by the added unit test but was not exercised against the live gateway.

## Tests

New test `carries the session store UUID on interactive diagnostic events`: drives a real interactive (Slack) dispatch with a store entry whose `sessionId` is `test-uuid-1234`, and asserts the emitted `logMessageQueued` and `logSessionStateChange` both carry `sessionId: "test-uuid-1234"`. Red before the change, green after. Full `dispatch-from-config.test.ts` suite remains green. (Supplemental to the live proof above.)

## Scope

Independent of #80497 (which adds `onModelDiagnosticEvent` at the delivery/filter layer in `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts`). This PR is at the emit/population layer (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) and touches disjoint files; the two can land in either order.

target.title: fix(discord): accumulate reasoning progress deltas
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #83983.

Discord progress-mode reasoning updates were formatting each reasoning callback with the display `Thinking...` wrapper before draft-preview merge logic saw it. Since the merge logic treats display-prefixed reasoning text as a full snapshot, Codex app-server delta chunks like `"Considering"`, `" plugin"`, `" installation"`, `"!"` could replace each other until only the final tiny chunk remained visible.

This keeps reasoning stream payloads raw until `pushReasoningProgress()` merges delta/snapshot semantics, then applies the existing reasoning display formatter once for the stable progress line. Follow-ups keep raw reasoning text that starts with `Thinking`, `Thinking:`, `Thinking...`, a single `Thinking` line, or `Reasoning:` intact, while still stripping legacy newline-prefixed `Reasoning:\n...` and `Thinking...\n\n_..._` display wrappers. Long reasoning progress lines are compacted before italic wrapping so Discord markdown remains balanced after truncation.

No config surface or plugin surface changes.

target.title: fix(update): detect npm shrinkwrap package installs
target.body:
## Summary
- treat `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as npm install evidence for published npm package roots, even when package.json carries build-time `packageManager: pnpm`
- preserve pnpm package-root ownership when pnpm global/project layout proves it owns a package root that also carries `npm-shrinkwrap.json`
- preserve pnpm sibling virtual-store roots shaped like `.../5/.pnpm/.../node_modules/openclaw` by mapping them back to the owning `.../5/node_modules` layout
- preserve Bun global package-root ownership even when the published package metadata still says `pnpm`
- use `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as the npm dependency lock marker when present so `openclaw status --json` does not report a missing pnpm/package-lock marker

Fixes #87732.

## Surface area
- No public config changes.
- No plugin API or plugin contract changes.
- No update command behavior rewrite; this only changes status/install detection and dependency marker reporting.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
A package-style OpenClaw install that contains `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, `node_modules`, and build-time package metadata should be reported using the package manager that owns the install root. npm shrinkwrap package roots should report npm, while pnpm and Bun-owned package roots should remain pnpm or Bun even when the published package also contains shrinkwrap and `packageManager: pnpm`.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu checkout at `/tmp/openclaw-pr-87778-refresh`, PR head `fd8dd4fd0ced`, source-level temp package roots using the real `detectPackageManager` and `checkUpdateStatus` code paths.

Exact command run after this patch:
`node --import tsx ./.proof-87778.ts`

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "status": "pass",
  "head": "fd8dd4fd0ced",
  "managers": {
    "npm": {
      "detected": "npm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "npm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "npm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "npm-shrinkwrap.json",
        "marker": "node_modules"
      }
    },
    "pnpmDirect": {
      "detected": "pnpm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "pnpm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "pnpm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "pnpm-lock.yaml",
        "marker": ".modules.yaml"
      }
    },
    "pnpmSiblingStore": {
      "detected": "pnpm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "pnpm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "pnpm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "pnpm-lock.yaml",
        "marker": ".modules.yaml"
      }
    },
    "bun": {
      "detected": "bun",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "bun",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "bun",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "bun.lockb",
        "marker": "node_modules"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The npm temp package root detected npm and used `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as the npm lockfile. The direct pnpm global package root detected pnpm. The pnpm sibling virtual-store root `.../6/.pnpm/openclaw@1.0.0/node_modules/openclaw` also detected pnpm by using the owning `.../6/node_modules` markers. The Bun-owned temp package root used published `packageManager: pnpm` metadata plus `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, but still detected Bun from the Bun global install root and used Bun dependency markers.

What was not tested:
No registry lookup was tested; this proof disables registry access with `includeRegistry: false` because the bug is local install/dependency-marker detection.

## Validation
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/detect-package-manager.test.ts src/infra/update-check.test.ts src/infra/update-global.test.ts src/commands/status.update.test.ts src/commands/status-json-payload.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 5 files, 74 tests passed

- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main` -> no actionable regressions found

target.title: fix(voice-call): avoid OpenAI realtime double greeting
target.body:
Fixes #85846

## Summary

- Keep OpenAI outbound queued initial greetings on the active explicit ready-trigger path so the provider sends `conversation.item.create` followed by `response.create` after `session.updated`.
- Fire the OpenAI `onReady()` callback before draining audio that arrived before `session.updated`, so queued initial greeting suppression reaches the Realtime session before pending caller audio can trigger server VAD.
- Suppress OpenAI Realtime server-VAD auto responses while a manual response is active, keep suppression in place while a pending retry is queued, then restore the configured auto-response behavior only after the manual turn finishes.
- Preserve base realtime session instructions instead of moving the queued greeting into `session.update` instructions.
- Keep non-OpenAI realtime providers on the existing explicit on-ready initial greeting path.
- No config surface or plugin API surface changed.

## Behavior proof

The focused regressions cover Omar's active-response requirement and ClawSweeper's pre-ready caller-audio blocker:

- `createBridge(...).instructions` remains the base session instructions (`"Be helpful."`), so the initial greeting is not passively parked in `session.update`.
- `onReady()` calls `triggerGreeting()` exactly once with the full queued first-turn instruction.
- The OpenAI provider regression proves `triggerGreeting()` sends one `conversation.item.create`, temporarily updates turn detection to `create_response:false`, sends one `response.create`, and only then drains pre-ready caller audio.
- A response-error regression proves active-response errors keep suppression active while a pending manual response retry is queued, and restore `create_response:true` after the retried response finishes.

## Verification

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 2 files, 72 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.ts extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts`
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.ts extensions/openai/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.ts extensions/voice-call/src/webhook/realtime-handler.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main` -> no actionable correctness issues found

Signed head: `57f4415f033b88ac209eeb21966ba4ea7e230d3b`.

Known proof gap: no live Twilio/OpenAI outbound call was run in this environment.

target.title: fix(update): detect npm shrinkwrap package installs
target.body:
## Summary
- treat `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as npm install evidence for published npm package roots, even when package.json carries build-time `packageManager: pnpm`
- preserve pnpm package-root ownership when pnpm global/project layout proves it owns a package root that also carries `npm-shrinkwrap.json`
- preserve pnpm sibling virtual-store roots shaped like `.../5/.pnpm/.../node_modules/openclaw` by mapping them back to the owning `.../5/node_modules` layout
- preserve Bun global package-root ownership even when the published package metadata still says `pnpm`
- use `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as the npm dependency lock marker when present so `openclaw status --json` does not report a missing pnpm/package-lock marker

Fixes #87732.

## Surface area
- No public config changes.
- No plugin API or plugin contract changes.
- No update command behavior rewrite; this only changes status/install detection and dependency marker reporting.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
A package-style OpenClaw install that contains `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, `node_modules`, and build-time package metadata should be reported using the package manager that owns the install root. npm shrinkwrap package roots should report npm, while pnpm and Bun-owned package roots should remain pnpm or Bun even when the published package also contains shrinkwrap and `packageManager: pnpm`.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu checkout at `/tmp/openclaw-pr-87778-refresh`, PR head `fd8dd4fd0ced`, source-level temp package roots using the real `detectPackageManager` and `checkUpdateStatus` code paths.

Exact command run after this patch:
`node --import tsx ./.proof-87778.ts`

Evidence after fix:
```json
{
  "status": "pass",
  "head": "fd8dd4fd0ced",
  "managers": {
    "npm": {
      "detected": "npm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "npm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "npm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "npm-shrinkwrap.json",
        "marker": "node_modules"
      }
    },
    "pnpmDirect": {
      "detected": "pnpm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "pnpm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "pnpm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "pnpm-lock.yaml",
        "marker": ".modules.yaml"
      }
    },
    "pnpmSiblingStore": {
      "detected": "pnpm",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "pnpm",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "pnpm",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "pnpm-lock.yaml",
        "marker": ".modules.yaml"
      }
    },
    "bun": {
      "detected": "bun",
      "installKind": "package",
      "packageManager": "bun",
      "deps": {
        "manager": "bun",
        "status": "ok",
        "lockfile": "bun.lockb",
        "marker": "node_modules"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix:
The npm temp package root detected npm and used `npm-shrinkwrap.json` as the npm lockfile. The direct pnpm global package root detected pnpm. The pnpm sibling virtual-store root `.../6/.pnpm/openclaw@1.0.0/node_modules/openclaw` also detected pnpm by using the owning `.../6/node_modules` markers. The Bun-owned temp package root used published `packageManager: pnpm` metadata plus `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, but still detected Bun from the Bun global install root and used Bun dependency markers.

What was not tested:
No registry lookup was tested; this proof disables registry access with `includeRegistry: false` because the bug is local install/dependency-marker detection.

## Validation
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/detect-package-manager.test.ts src/infra/update-check.test.ts src/infra/update-global.test.ts src/commands/status.update.test.ts src/commands/status-json-payload.test.ts --reporter=dot` -> 5 files, 74 tests passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/infra/detect-package-manager.ts src/infra/detect-package-manager.test.ts` -> passed
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig ./tsconfig.json src/infra/detect-package-manager.ts src/infra/detect-package-manager.test.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `git diff --check`
- `codex review --base origin/main` -> no actionable regressions found

Rebased onto current `main` (`e18099b8c32420fa2ff5eddf8731e24c7278851b`). All 5 commits are signed. Signed head: `fd8dd4fd0cedf6abc398dad39dec8f332062da5b`.

target.title: feat(deepseek): show provider balance in usage status
target.body:
## What

- Registers DeepSeek as a provider usage source and wires the DeepSeek provider plugin to resolve API-key usage auth.
- Adds a DeepSeek balance fetcher for `GET /user/balance`, exposed through the plugin SDK provider-usage subpath.
- Adds `ProviderUsageSnapshot.summary` so balance-only providers can report provider text without pretending it is a quota window.
- Shows summary-only usage in `/status`, status reports, and the models auth status usage enrichment for DeepSeek API-key profiles.

## Why

After the provider plugin architecture moved provider-specific usage logic into plugins, DeepSeek was not wired into usage snapshots. Users could see local session costs, but `/status` and related status surfaces could not show the DeepSeek account balance.

DeepSeek balance is not a reset-window quota, so this keeps it as provider-reported summary text instead of fabricating a percent-left window.

## Public Contract Rationale

- `ProviderUsageSnapshot.summary` is optional and additive. Existing providers with quota windows keep using `windows`; shared formatters only fall back to `summary` when a provider has no windows and no error.
- `models.authStatus.usage.summary` is optional and mirrors the same snapshot field so API-key balance providers can expose account-state text without changing existing window payloads.
- The provider-usage SDK export keeps DeepSeek-specific HTTP parsing inside the bundled DeepSeek provider path instead of requiring plugins to import internal `src` modules.
- Tests cover summary-only formatting, DeepSeek auth-status enrichment, provider loading, and existing window-based formatting behavior.

## Impact

DeepSeek API-key users can now see balance text such as `Balance ¥42.50` in usage/status surfaces. Existing quota-window providers continue to use `windows`; summary-only formatting is only used when no windows are present.

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: DeepSeek API-key usage snapshots now return provider balance text and the summary-only formatter displays it instead of dropping providers with no quota windows.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout of this PR branch at `0d31dbc6ace5545c478f399ce5bb1ddc746fe6df`, Node/tsx runtime, real DeepSeek API key from local secret storage. Secret and balance amount were redacted from the captured output.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local Node/tsx snippet that imports `fetchDeepSeekUsage` from `src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.deepseek.ts`, wraps `fetch` to capture only safe request metadata, calls the live DeepSeek `https://api.deepseek.com/user/balance` endpoint with the real API key, then passes the returned snapshot through `formatUsageWindowSummary`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
DeepSeek usage live proof
head=0d31dbc6ace5545c478f399ce5bb1ddc746fe6df
endpoint=https://api.deepseek.com/user/balance
method=GET
authorizationHeaderPresent=true
accept=application/json
provider=deepseek
displayName=DeepSeek
windows=0
hasSummary=true
summary=Balance ¥<redacted>
formatted=Balance ¥<redacted>
error=none
```

- Observed result after fix: The live DeepSeek response produced a `ProviderUsageSnapshot` with `provider=deepseek`, zero quota windows, a non-empty balance summary, and no error. The formatter returned the same redacted balance text instead of `null`.
- What was not tested: Full gateway UI/dashboard manual click path and invalid-key live error handling were not exercised in this live proof. The exact balance amount is intentionally redacted.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: This uses a real DeepSeek account balance endpoint, but the local account balance value cannot be shared publicly. Automated unit tests cover the mocked error and formatting edge cases separately.
- Before evidence: Before this patch, DeepSeek was absent from `usageProviders` and had no provider usage hook, so `/status` and usage surfaces had no DeepSeek balance snapshot to format.

## Validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.deepseek.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.format.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/models-auth-status.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-status.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/concepts/usage-tracking.md docs/plugins/sdk-subpaths.md`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental false --pretty false`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.deepseek.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.format.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts extensions/deepseek/index.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/models-auth-status.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-status.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn-applies-thinking-default.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-guard-coverage.test.ts`
- `pnpm deadcode:unused-files`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/bash-tools.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Current Branch Note

This branch was refreshed onto current `upstream/main` (`a71b121c69`) in merge commit `0d31dbc6ace5545c478f399ce5bb1ddc746fe6df`; follow-up commits `2aa4a69943`, `797c52ea95`, `c173336f90`, and `73a99da308` align subagent config typing, clarify usage docs, aggregate every parseable DeepSeek balance entry, and fix current-main CI drift in Vite hook typing, dynamic alias deadcode scanning, and bash-tools shell snapshot tests.

A local `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`/commit hook attempt was blocked by registry fetch timeouts while installing newly introduced upstream binary packages. The SDK baseline was regenerated and checked directly with `node --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts`; GitHub CI will re-run the full matrix on the pushed merge head.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, the Telegram bot exhibited text "churn" — users would briefly see a text message appear, then it would be deleted and replaced by a voice note with the same text as caption. This happened because two independent delivery paths were active simultaneously:

1. **Draft stream path**: `onPartialReply` → `bot.api.sendMessage()` sent preview text immediately
2. **ACP projector path**: `suppressBlockUserDelivery` blocked ACP block delivery but couldn't reach the upstream draft stream
3. **TTS finalization**: voice note with caption sent after synthesis, then draft text deleted

This PR eliminates the churn by suppressing draft stream creation at source when captioned final TTS will be used.

## Root cause

```
Before (main):
  1. onPartialReply fires → draft stream creates sendMessage preview → user sees text
  2. TTS synthesis runs (2-5 seconds)
  3. Voice note + caption delivered → draft text deleted
  Result: user sees text flash then disappear = churn (#83511)

After (this PR):
  1. resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan() → willUseCaptionedFinalTts = true
  2. canStreamAnswerDraft = false → NO draft stream created
  3. TTS synthesis runs silently
  4. Voice note + caption delivered as single message
  Result: user sees only the voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/tts/captioned-final-tts.ts` | **New** shared helper `resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan()` combining channel capability, TTS mode, and TTS status checks |
| `src/tts/captioned-final-tts.test.ts` | **New** 4 unit tests for the shared helper |
| `src/plugin-sdk/tts-runtime.ts` | Re-export `resolveCaptionedFinalTtsPlan` for extension access |
| `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` | Updated SDK API baseline hash |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` | Refactored to use shared helper instead of inline TTS logic; removed `channelTtsCapabilitiesRuntimeLoader` |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 regression tests for deferred settlement, block suppression, abort fallback |
| `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` | Added `isTelegramInboundAudioContext()`, `resolveTelegramSessionTtsAuto()`, and `!willUseCaptionedFinalTts` guard on `canStreamAnswerDraft` |
| `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` | 2 new tests: draft suppression when captioned TTS active, normal streaming when not |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText` capability declaration |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts` | Test for capability declaration |
| `src/channels/plugins/types.core.ts` | JSDoc for `captionedFinalText` capability contract |
| `src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts` | Capability seam test |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/acp-projector.ts` | Block delivery suppression support |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.ts` | Delivery coordinator for suppressed text fallback |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ 58 passed (58)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tts/captioned-final-tts.test.ts
 ✓ 4 passed (4)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
 ✓ 96 passed (96)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
 ✓ 11 passed (11)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
 ✓ 1 passed (1)

$ pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check
 OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

Total: 170 tests passed across 5 test suites
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram TTS final-mode "delete-and-resend churn" — when TTS mode is `final`, users saw a text message flash briefly (2-5 seconds), then it disappeared and a voice note with caption appeared. Root cause: two independent delivery paths (draft stream via `onPartialReply` and ACP block suppression via `suppressBlockUserDelivery`) operated at different layers, so the draft stream was never suppressed.

- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout at commit `e33c474a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0. Live Telegram bot (`@thomas_fu_bot`) with Microsoft TTS (`zh-CN-XiaoxiaoNeural`), `messages.tts.mode: "final"`, `messages.tts.auto: "always"`, `channels.telegram.streaming.mode: "block"`.

- **Failing proof before fix (live Telegram)**:

  ![Before fix — separate text message + voice message (churn)](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/48AAB56AD0D1DC685CB3918F75AEB402)

  User sent "hello" to the Telegram bot. The bot responded with **two separate messages**: a text message ("老板好，好久不见！有什么需要效劳的？") AND a voice note — instead of a single voice note with caption. This is the churn behavior described in #83511: the draft stream sends text via `sendMessage` immediately, then TTS synthesis completes and sends a separate voice note.

- **Exact steps after this patch**:
  1. Applied patch (commit `e33c474a`) via `npm link`
  2. Restarted Gateway: `openclaw gateway restart`
  3. Sent messages from Telegram to the bot
  4. Observed: voice note with caption appeared directly — no text flash

- **Evidence after fix (live Telegram screenshot)**:

  ![After fix — voice messages with caption, no text churn](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/6937B66CC8A27C33B76FBD54155B542C)

  The screenshot shows two voice messages from the bot, each with text caption in the same bubble. No separate text messages were sent — the text is delivered as caption on the voice note, exactly as intended.

- **Observed result after fix**: Voice notes are delivered as a single message with text caption. No draft text message is created or deleted. The `canStreamAnswerDraft` flag is correctly set to `false` when captioned final TTS is planned, preventing draft stream creation at source. Non-TTS conversations continue to use normal streaming. Other channels (Discord, Slack, etc.) are unaffected.

- **What was not tested**: Other TTS providers (only Microsoft TTS tested live). Group chat TTS behavior (tested in DM only). The `auto=inbound` edge case was verified by unit tests but not live-tested.

Closes #83511


---

## Additional fix: dispatch-from-config text deferral + draft stream suppression (e33c474a)

The original fix addressed only `dispatch-acp.ts` and `bot-message-dispatch.ts`. Live testing revealed two additional issues:

### Issue 1: Missing text deferral in `dispatch-from-config.ts`
The user's agent used the `dispatch-from-config` path (not ACP), which lacked the `shouldDeferTextForCaptionedFinalTts` gate. Block text was delivered before TTS voice synthesis completed, causing split messages.

**Fix**: Added `shouldDeferTextForCaptionedFinalTts` gate in `dispatch-from-config.ts` with:
- Block text accumulation and deferral
- Final payload text merging when resolver returns text
- Deferred text recovery on abort/error paths via `routeReplyToOriginating`
- 8 targeted tests (189 total in dispatch-from-config.test.ts)

### Issue 2: Draft stream (`onPartialReply`) operating independently
The Telegram draft stream sent real-time text previews via `editMessageText` independently from block delivery suppression, causing text to flicker before voice+caption arrived.

**Fix**: Suppress `onPartialReply` when `shouldDeferTextForCaptionedFinalTts` is active:
```typescript
onPartialReply: shouldDeferTextForCaptionedFinalTts
  ? undefined
  : wrapProgressCallback(params.replyOptions?.onPartialReply),
```

### Additional files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` | Text deferral gate + draft stream suppression |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts` | 9 new tests for deferral, merge, abort, error, routing, and draft suppression |
| `src/tts/tts-config.ts` | Type definitions for captioned TTS |
| `extensions/telegram/src/shared.ts` | `captionedFinalText: true` capability |
| `extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery-text-deliverer.ts` | TTS voice caption merge in lane deliverer |
| `extensions/telegram/src/lane-delivery.test.ts` | 2 new tests for lane deliverer merge path |

### Verified fix (e33c474a)

![After fix — single voice message with caption, no text flicker](https://cdn.deepminer.com.cn/im-test-xming/chat/5/f6138257d34c4372bf6b6143e44cb3f5____2060648631760850944/A1CC95B74A5340F384CAB69EDB482F34.jpg)

### Test results (all passing)

```
dispatch-from-config.test.ts:     189 passed
dispatch-from-config.acp-abort:    16 passed
dispatch-acp.test.ts:              59 passed
lane-delivery.test.ts:             34 passed
Total:                            298 passed
```

commands.author: stevenepalmer

target.title: fix(configure): migrate stale Codex defaults after OpenAI auth
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes a configure/auth mismatch where OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex auth could preserve a stale legacy `codex/gpt-5.5` default while adding the canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` model.
- Keeps the selected primary model aligned with the canonical OpenAI provider id returned by auth.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #88456 reports successful ChatGPT/Codex device pairing followed by config returning to the menu with an unusable default model outside the selected provider model set.

What is the intended outcome?

- After OpenAI/Codex auth returns `openai/gpt-5.5`, a matching stale `codex/gpt-5.5` or `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` primary is migrated to `openai/gpt-5.5` instead of being preserved.
- Existing default preservation is unchanged for unrelated providers.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not redesign the OpenAI/Codex model picker or change live OAuth/device-code transport.
- This does not remove legacy model-map entries; it only prevents a matching stale legacy ref from remaining the primary default.

What does success look like?

- The configured primary model is present under the canonical OpenAI model entry after auth.
- Existing non-OpenAI defaults are still preserved when provider auth only adds another available model.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The preserve-existing-default guard in `src/plugins/provider-auth-choice.ts`.
- The regression test proving `codex/gpt-5.5` migrates to `openai/gpt-5.5` only for the matching OpenAI auth path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #88456

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #88456

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No direct maintainer request beyond the linked issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex auth no longer leaves a matching stale legacy Codex ref as the default primary while canonical OpenAI refs are added to the model map.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on this branch, invoking the actual provider-auth config application path with a redacted local token/provider setup.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the auth-choice config application path directly with before primary `codex/gpt-5.5`, selected auth model `openai/gpt-5.5`, and `preserveExistingDefaultModel: true`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.cache/corepack PNPM_HOME=/home/node/.local/share/pnpm node --import tsx --input-type=module
  {
    "beforePrimary": "codex/gpt-5.5",
    "afterPrimary": "openai/gpt-5.5",
    "allowlistKeys": [
      "codex/gpt-5.5",
      "openai/gpt-5.5"
    ],
    "note": {
      "title": "Model configured",
      "message": "Default model set to openai/gpt-5.5"
    }
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The primary default changes to canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` while both legacy and canonical model entries remain present in `agents.defaults.models`.
- What was not tested: A full live ChatGPT/Codex device-code browser pairing against a real account from this container.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The worker cannot complete the interactive account authorization step without a user/browser handoff. The proof exercises the same config mutation path after auth returns a canonical OpenAI default.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The linked issue shows the broken state: primary `codex/gpt-5.5` with canonical OpenAI entries in the model allowlist.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.prompt-auth-config.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/provider-auth-choice.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added provider-auth coverage for `preserveExistingDefaultModel: true` with existing primary `codex/gpt-5.5` and OpenAI auth default `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- The test verifies the primary is migrated to `openai/gpt-5.5`, the canonical model entry is present, and the provider selection hook still runs.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The stale legacy default could be preserved as primary even though OpenAI auth added canonical `openai/*` entries, leaving the session default outside the selected provider allowlist.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; regression coverage was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

- The preserve-existing-default behavior during provider auth.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The new exception is narrow: it only applies when OpenAI auth returns an `openai/*` model and the previous primary is a matching legacy `codex/*` or `openai-codex/*` ref for the same model id.
- Existing coverage still verifies unrelated existing defaults remain preserved.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI.
- Optional maintainer/live-environment confirmation of the full ChatGPT/Codex device-code prompt flow.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Initial Real behavior proof gate failure was addressed by replacing prose-only evidence with copied terminal output.

target.title: fix(configure): migrate stale Codex defaults after OpenAI auth
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Fixes a configure/auth mismatch where OpenAI ChatGPT/Codex auth could preserve a stale legacy `codex/gpt-5.5` default while adding the canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` model.
- Keeps the selected primary model aligned with the canonical OpenAI provider id returned by auth.

Why does this matter now?

- Issue #88456 reports successful ChatGPT/Codex device pairing followed by config returning to the menu with an unusable default model outside the selected provider model set.

What is the intended outcome?

- After OpenAI/Codex auth returns `openai/gpt-5.5`, a matching stale `codex/gpt-5.5` or `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` primary is migrated to `openai/gpt-5.5` instead of being preserved.
- Existing default preservation is unchanged for unrelated providers.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- This does not redesign the OpenAI/Codex model picker or change live OAuth/device-code transport.
- This does not remove legacy model-map entries; it only prevents a matching stale legacy ref from remaining the primary default.

What does success look like?

- The configured primary model is present under the canonical OpenAI model entry after auth.
- Existing non-OpenAI defaults are still preserved when provider auth only adds another available model.

What should reviewers focus on?

- The preserve-existing-default guard in `src/plugins/provider-auth-choice.ts`.
- The regression test proving `codex/gpt-5.5` migrates to `openai/gpt-5.5` only for the matching OpenAI auth path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #88456

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #88456

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No direct maintainer request beyond the linked issue.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenAI/Codex auth no longer leaves a matching stale legacy Codex ref as the default primary while canonical OpenAI refs are added to the model map.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on this branch, invoking the actual provider-auth config application path with a redacted local token/provider setup.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the auth-choice config application path directly with before primary `codex/gpt-5.5`, selected auth model `openai/gpt-5.5`, and `preserveExistingDefaultModel: true`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  Terminal transcript:
  ```text
  $ COREPACK_HOME=/home/node/.cache/corepack PNPM_HOME=/home/node/.local/share/pnpm node --import tsx --input-type=module
  {
    "beforePrimary": "codex/gpt-5.5",
    "afterPrimary": "openai/gpt-5.5",
    "allowlistKeys": [
      "codex/gpt-5.5",
      "openai/gpt-5.5"
    ],
    "note": {
      "title": "Model configured",
      "message": "Default model set to openai/gpt-5.5"
    }
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The primary default changes to canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` while both legacy and canonical model entries remain present in `agents.defaults.models`.
- What was not tested: A full live ChatGPT/Codex device-code browser pairing against a real account from this container.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The worker cannot complete the interactive account authorization step without a user/browser handoff. The proof exercises the same config mutation path after auth returns a canonical OpenAI default.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The linked issue shows the broken state: primary `codex/gpt-5.5` with canonical OpenAI entries in the model allowlist.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.commands.config.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.prompt-auth-config.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/provider-auth-choice.ts src/commands/auth-choice.apply.plugin-provider.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added provider-auth coverage for `preserveExistingDefaultModel: true` with existing primary `codex/gpt-5.5` and OpenAI auth default `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- The test verifies the primary is migrated to `openai/gpt-5.5`, the canonical model entry is present, and the provider selection hook still runs.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The stale legacy default could be preserved as primary even though OpenAI auth added canonical `openai/*` entries, leaving the session default outside the selected provider allowlist.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; regression coverage was added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

- The preserve-existing-default behavior during provider auth.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The new exception is narrow: it only applies when OpenAI auth returns an `openai/*` model and the previous primary is a matching legacy `codex/*` or `openai-codex/*` ref for the same model id.
- Existing coverage still verifies unrelated existing defaults remain preserved.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI.
- Optional maintainer/live-environment confirmation of the full ChatGPT/Codex device-code prompt flow.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Initial Real behavior proof gate failure was addressed by replacing prose-only evidence with copied terminal output.

target.title: fix(plugins): block untrusted workspace setup-only channel loads
target.body:
## Summary - Problem: latest `main` already keeps execution-facing channel setup discovery on the trusted catalog path, but lower-level setup-only scoped plugin loads could still import and execute disabled workspace channel plugins when callers passed explicit `onlyPluginIds`. - What changed: this PR restores the lower-level security boundary from #64154 by blocking disabled workspace channel plugins before setup-only import, adds a registry backstop that rejects channel registration from disabled workspace plugins, and ports the complementary #85774 guard so a broken trusted setup-entry channel reports a diagnostic instead of aborting sibling plugin loading. - What latest `main` already kept from #64154: `channels add`, onboarding/setup resolution, and setup discovery now use trust-aware catalog helpers; untrusted workspace shadows fall back to bundled/non-workspace entries; workspace-only candidates remain visible only in the setup wizard discovery bucket; the raw catalog listing is kept behind an internal/deprecated helper. - Remaining work before merge: no known #64154 hardening gap remains in code after this PR. The remaining gates are CI plus any maintainer-requested live external-plugin proof. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Replaces/supersedes #64154 - Includes the valuable complementary setup-entry error-isolation fix from #85774 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Current Main vs This PR The #64154 design had two layers: 1. Catalog callers should resolve channels through trusted helpers so setup/add flows do not select untrusted workspace shadows. 2. The loader/registry should enforce the same trust boundary before any plugin module can execute, so lower-level callers cannot bypass the catalog layer. Latest `main` still preserves the first layer. `channels add`, setup/onboarding, and channel setup plugin resolution use `listTrustedChannelPluginCatalogEntries` / `getTrustedChannelPluginCatalogEntry`; the setup wizard's installable display can still show workspace-only candidates through the lenient discovery helper without treating them as executable/installed. This PR fills the second layer. It prevents explicit setup-only scoped loads from importing disabled workspace channel plugins, rejects disabled workspace channel registrations at the registry boundary, and keeps trusted workspace overrides working. It also ports #85774's try/catch around `api.registerChannel(mergedSetupPlugin)`, which addresses a related but distinct resilience gap: trusted setup-entry plugins are allowed to load, but one malformed registration should not stop healthy sibling plugins from loading. ## Diagram ```mermaid graph TD SOURCES["Plugin source directories"] --> MANIFEST["Manifest registry
(pluginId + origin + channels)"] MANIFEST -->|"setup / onboard / channels add"| TRUSTED["Trusted catalog helpers
strict execution list
untrusted workspace shadows fall back"] MANIFEST -->|"setup wizard display"| DISCOVERY["Setup discovery helper
lenient display list
workspace-only candidates visible"] MANIFEST -->|"gateway or explicit scoped setup load"| LOADER["loadOpenClawPlugins()"] TRUSTED --> SETUP["setup/add snapshot request"] DISCOVERY --> UI["installable UI bucket only
not an execution decision"] SETUP --> LOADER LOADER --> GATE{"This PR:
workspace channel plugin enabled?"} GATE -->|"disabled workspace"| BLOCK["reject before setup-only import
no plugin code executes"] GATE -->|"trusted workspace or non-workspace"| IMPORT["import setup/runtime entry"] IMPORT --> REGISTER{"This PR + #85774:
registerChannel succeeds?"} REGISTER -->|"yes"| REGISTRY["snapshot/runtime registry"] REGISTER -->|"throws"| DIAG["record plugin diagnostic
continue loading siblings"] REGISTRY --> SNAPUSE["validateInput / applyAccountConfig
write config / lifecycle callbacks"] REGISTRY --> RUNTIME["gateway/runtime channel lookup"] BLOCK:::fixed GATE:::fixed DIAG:::fixed TRUSTED:::kept DISCOVERY:::display UI:::display classDef fixed fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px classDef kept fill:#d9edf7,stroke:#31708f,stroke-width:2px classDef display fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px ``` The important boundary is now shared: catalog helpers decide which channel catalog entries are trusted for setup/add flows, and the loader/registry enforce the same rule before runtime code can execute. If a trusted setup-entry channel still fails during registration, the error is isolated to that plugin and sibling plugins continue loading. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: untrusted workspace channel plugins should not execute during setup-only scoped loads; disabled workspace channel registrations should be rejected at registry assembly; trusted setup-entry channel registration failures should not abort sibling plugin loading. - Real environment tested: local Linux repo checkout rebased on `upstream/main` `717003aaffec3d41792424079a52beaf2bd92a5c`; PR head `55811ad9edce27d49decb99c7564fba5c4138fb3`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: - `pnpm docs:list` - `pnpm test src/commands/onboard-channels.e2e.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - `pnpm test src/plugins/loader.test.ts src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/channel-plugin-resolution.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/discovery.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/workspace-shadow-bypass.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/plugin-install.test.ts src/commands/channels.add.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` - `pnpm build` - `pnpm check:changed` - Evidence after fix: `src/commands/onboard-channels.e2e.test.ts` passes 16/16 after exposing `listRawChannelPluginCatalogEntries` through the same mock/actual fallback as the old catalog helper; the focused loader/registry/catalog/channel setup tests pass after the rebase; `pnpm build` and the final `pnpm check:changed` pass; `CHANGELOG.md` and stale transcripts tool-display metadata are no longer in the final branch diff. - Observed result after fix: disabled workspace shadows no longer execute through scoped setup-only loads; trusted workspace overrides remain available; disabled workspace channel registrations are rejected as a registry backstop; malformed trusted setup-entry channel registration records a diagnostic and sibling plugins keep loading. - What was not tested: live channel setup against a real external workspace plugin outside the loader test harness. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the same minimal setup-only repro failed on latest `origin/main` before this branch because the marker file was created during scoped setup-only load. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: the scoped setup-only loader path treated explicit `onlyPluginIds` as sufficient to load a disabled workspace channel plugin, so workspace trust was bypassed below the trusted-catalog call sites. - Missing detection / guardrail: registry assembly still accepted channel registration from disabled workspace plugins if a lower-level caller imported one. - Complementary #85774 root cause: the setup-entry loader loop caught surrounding registration failures, but `api.registerChannel(mergedSetupPlugin)` itself was unguarded, so one throwing trusted channel registration could abort sibling plugin loading. - Contributing context: latest `main` already absorbed the higher-level `channels add` / trusted-catalog call-site fixes from #64154, but not this lower-level loader/registry backstop or the setup-entry error-isolation guard. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `src/plugins/loader.test.ts`, `src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts`, `src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts`, and existing channel setup/add tests. - Scenario the test should lock in: explicit scoped setup-only loads must reject disabled workspace channel plugins but keep trusted workspace channel plugins available; registry assembly must reject disabled workspace channel registrations; trusted setup-entry registration errors must be recorded without aborting healthy sibling plugins. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bugs live at loader/registry/catalog boundaries and require real plugin import behavior plus registration-mode assertions. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): `only setup-loads a disabled channel plugin when the caller scopes to the selected plugin` still covers the non-workspace path. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - Untrusted workspace channel shadows no longer execute during setup-only scoped loads. - Trusted workspace channel plugins remain available to setup-time scoped loading. - A broken trusted setup-entry channel registration now surfaces as a plugin diagnostic instead of stopping sibling plugin loading. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Linux - Runtime/container: Node 22 local repo checkout - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader / channel plugin registry / channel setup catalog - Relevant config (redacted): workspace plugins enabled; untrusted plugin omitted from allowlist ### Steps 1. Create a workspace channel plugin fixture with `channels` metadata and a side-effect marker write in `register()`. 2. Load it through `loadOpenClawPlugins({ includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true, forceSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true, onlyPluginIds: [...] })`. 3. Compare disabled workspace behavior against trusted workspace behavior. 4. Load a setup-entry channel whose channel config getter throws during `registerChannel` alongside a healthy sibling plugin. ### Expected - Disabled workspace channel plugin stays disabled and does not execute. - Trusted workspace channel plugin remains available to setup-only loading. - A throwing trusted setup-entry channel records a diagnostic and does not prevent sibling plugins from loading. ### Actual - This branch matches the expected behavior. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets in test output - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: untrusted/trusted workspace scoped setup-only regressions, disabled workspace registry guard, trusted-catalog filtering semantics, onboarding channel setup e2e catalog mock compatibility, `channels add` workspace shadow fallback, #85774 setup-entry `registerChannel` error isolation, `pnpm build`, and final `pnpm check:changed`. - Edge cases checked: trusted workspace override still loads in setup-only mode; non-workspace disabled plugin behavior stays unchanged; setup wizard discovery still keeps workspace-only install candidates visible without treating them as installed/executable. - What you did **not** verify: a live end-to-end channel setup outside the loader test harness. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) No. This is an intentional security hardening break for disabled workspace channel plugins that were previously reachable through setup-only scoped loads. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No automatic migration. Operators who intentionally develop or run a workspace/linked channel plugin must explicitly enable/trust that plugin before setup-time code can run. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: use the normal plugin enable/trust flow for the workspace plugin before running channel setup. The behavior is documented in `docs/cli/plugins.md`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: a trusted workspace plugin could accidentally get blocked if the trust check were too broad. - Mitigation: regression coverage explicitly keeps the trusted workspace scoped-load path green. - Risk: setup discovery could accidentally hide workspace-only install candidates. - Mitigation: trusted-catalog and discovery tests keep the strict execution list separate from the lenient setup wizard display list. - Risk: an existing disabled workspace channel plugin that relied on setup-only execution now fails closed. - Mitigation: this is the intended security behavior; the docs now state that workspace-origin plugins are not imported or executed until explicitly enabled/trusted. ### Proof 1: Pre-Fix Reproduction on origin/main - Behavior addressed: Untrusted workspace channel plugin (evi1-channel-probe) executes its register() function via the setup-only scoped load path when includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins: true is passed to loadOpenClawPlugins, bypassing the trust boundary. - Real environment tested: Crabbox docker, provider=docker, container=391883b7652bcb464c523f64ddcabb9bcad47f92e434737b7cc5391f7bc0922f, run=cbx_6dadb5d505eb, checkout SHA=ead847f6065ac36031f9e7a95dd1ee90f171f79c (origin/main) - Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) `git fetch --depth=1 origin main && git checkout FETCH_HEAD` inside Crabbox container; (2) `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`; (3) Deploy evi1-channel-probe plugin to /tmp/proof1-workspace/.openclaw/extensions/evi1-channel-probe/; (4) Confirm /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence absent; (5) Run `node probe-untrusted.mjs` with onlyPluginIds=["evi1-channel-probe"], includeSetupOnlyChannelPlugins=true, activate=false, cache=false; (6) Check /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence existence. - Evidence after fix: Marker file /tmp/openclaw-marker-evidence CREATED with content "evi1-probe-executed\n". Plugin status="disabled" with channelSetups=[{id:"evi1-channel-probe"}] — the setup-only path imported and executed the untrusted workspace plugin code. - Observed result after fix: On origin/main (pre-fix), the untrusted workspace channel plugin code ran despite being disabled. The plugin appeared in channelSetups (not channels), status was "disabled", yet register() executed and created the marker file. This confirms the bypass vulnerability. - What was not tested: CLI blackbox path, real channels add flow ### Proof 2: Post-Fix Verification on PR Head - Behavior addressed: The PR fix blocks untrusted workspace channel plugins from executing during setup-only scoped loads; disabled workspace channel registrations are rejected at registry assembly; trusted workspace plugins with plugins.allow still work; broken trusted setup-entry registerChannel records a diagnostic without aborting sibling plugins. - Real environment tested: Crabbox docker, provider=docker, container=df29879e2faef7ac8794cba519c1988aff410316a3718d90c6f638cd9977ce15, lease=cbx_0f687f5da081, checkout SHA=b05a7b14fe9bd6c119b3b7fe60a1967d0987804c (PR head at time of test; refreshed #86953 head is `2a9a2d79fd498be5fc62e83634a544f873b67b4c`) - Exact steps or command run after this patch: (1) `git fetch origin pull/82579/head:pr-head && git checkout pr-head` inside Crabbox container; (2) `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile && pnpm build`; (3) `grep -r "channel registration rejected for disabled workspace plugin" dist/` — fix code found in dist/loader-DM-dphfh.js; (4) Deploy evi1-channel-probe (untrusted), confirm marker ABSENT after probe; (5) Deploy evi1-trusted-probe (with plugins.allow), confirm marker EXISTS; (6) Deploy evi1-broken-register + evi1-sibling-probe (with plugins.allow), confirm sibling marker EXISTS and diagnostic recorded; (7) Run focused tests: `pnpm test src/plugins/loader.test.ts` (142 pass), `pnpm test src/plugins/registry.channel-guard.test.ts` (2 pass), `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/trusted-catalog.test.ts` (5 pass), `pnpm test src/commands/channel-setup/workspace-shadow-bypass.test.ts` (6 pass); (8) `pnpm check:changed` — 0 errors. - Evidence after fix: Untrusted probe: MARKER_ABSENT, exit code 0 (clean block, not crash). Plugin status="disabled", channelSetups=[]. Trusted probe: MARKER_EXISTS, content "evi1-probe-executed\n". Plugin status="loaded", channels and channelSetups populated. Error isolation: broken-register records diagnostic (level=error, pluginId=evi1-broken-register), process exit code 0. Sibling marker EXISTS, content "evi1-sibling-probe-executed\n". - Observed result after fix: The fix blocks untrusted workspace channel plugin execution. Untrusted plugin stays disabled with empty channelSetups. Trusted workspace plugin with plugins.allow still loads and registers channels. Broken trusted registerChannel records a diagnostic and sibling plugins continue loading. All focused tests pass. - What was not tested: CLI blackbox path, real channels add flow, 3 pre-existing test failures in channel-auth.test.ts (2) and channels.remove.test.ts (1) unrelated to this PR Crabbox cross-reference: Proof 1 provider=docker, container=391883b7652bcb464c523f64ddcabb9bcad47f92e434737b7cc5391f7bc0922f, run=cbx_6dadb5d505eb, SHA=ead847f6065ac36031f9e7a95dd1ee90f171f79c (origin/main). target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`
